source_pipeline: pipeline/seeds/sf_timeline_{reference,archive}.json (BLS CPI-U + Census + in-session Internet Archive OCR curation) + Internet Archive _djvu.txt (re-verified) + mart_us_sf_budget_by_year → export_sf_timeline.py

value_definitions.value_nominal: The figure exactly as printed in the scan (archive) or adopted budget (live) — the untouched anchor, never altered or interpolated.
value_definitions.value_real_today: value_nominal adjusted for inflation to 2025 dollars with the CPI-U deflator below — a labeled derived transform.
value_definitions.value_real_per_capita: value_real_today divided by that year's SF population — a labeled derived transform.

deflator_source.index: US CPI-U (All Urban Consumers, U.S. city average, All items)
deflator_source.source: 1913-2025: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, CPI-U series CUUR0000SA0, annual average. Pre-1913: Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis long-run CPI estimate (Warren-Pearson / historical splice, 1967=100), rescaled to the 1982-84=100 base by chain-splicing at 1913.

population_source.series: City and County of San Francisco, U.S. decennial Census population
population_source.source: U.S. Census Bureau, decennial census counts for the City and County of San Francisco.
population_source.method: Decennial census counts. Intercensal years for a timeline point are linearly interpolated between the two bracketing censuses; years after the latest census use that census count.

live_source.name: Budget
live_source.perimeter: Adopted budget (Annual Appropriation Ordinance), all funds, net of transfers.

archive_note: Archive figures are the single citywide headline total as printed in a scanned San Francisco serial financial report on the Internet Archive, transcribed verbatim with its page-level deep link. The historical serials report different top-lines across eras (total receipts, total expenditures, total budget) — recorded per point in `measure` — so the archive tier is shown 'as reported in the scan' and never joined to the live line.
notes: Real dollars are adjusted with the CPI-U to 2025 (pre-1913: Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis historical index, spliced to the BLS CPI-U at 1913); comparisons across such spans mix price levels with a growing city and an expanding scope of government.

points.label: FY 1887-88
points.caption: Total amount of expenditures
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 1880 and 1890 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 1894-95
points.caption: Total receipts during the year (all funds, Auditor's statement)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 1890 and 1900 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 1912-13
points.caption: Total receipts (Auditor's statement of sources of revenue)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 1910 and 1920 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 1967-68
points.caption: Total budget including bond funds (revenue appropriations)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 1960 and 1970 decennial censuses
points.ocr_note: On this recapitulation page the balanced total is $500,282,171 (revenue appropriations = expenditure appropriations + receipts by transfer).

points.label: FY 1985-86
points.caption: Total budget, all fund groups (annual appropriation ordinance)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 1980 and 1990 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 2010
points.caption: Total city spending, adopted budget (all funds, net of transfers)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 2000 and 2010 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 2015
points.caption: Total city spending, adopted budget (all funds, net of transfers)
points.population_note: linearly interpolated between the 2010 and 2020 decennial censuses

points.label: FY 2020
points.caption: Total city spending, adopted budget (all funds, net of transfers)

points.label: FY 2025
points.caption: Total city spending, adopted budget (all funds, net of transfers)

modern.narrative: Between FY2010 and FY2025, adopted city spending rose from $6.7B to $15.9B. The largest single increase was in Public Works, Transportation & Commerce (+$3.6B, to $5.9B), and the health and human-services areas grew fastest — Human Welfare & Neighborhood Development more than 3× its FY2010 level (+$1.9B). Personnel — salaries and mandatory fringe benefits — went from $3.5B to $7.0B, roughly 2.0× over the span.
modern.note: Computed from the SF adopted-budget marts. Compared on service areas and personnel only — the FY2018 PeopleSoft chart-of-accounts change relabels finer spending types, so those are not comparable before and after and are left out here.

archive.framing: illustrative lines from the ledger (verbatim), not a ranking

composition.archive_note: What the city funded in the archival era, transcribed verbatim from the scanned reports (page-level deep links). 1888 lines are illustrative ledger entries, not a ranking; 1913 are department totals as printed. Fuller per-era breakdowns are a work in progress.
